cataloging_subject.label: Life and Translation of Saint Thomas of Canterbury
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the medieval Middle English Becket material represented by Hermann Thiemke's 1919 critical edition and encoded as CME00142. It contains the Life of Saint Thomas of Canterbury in two alternative opening redactions (Thiemke's birth-story versions a and b) followed by a shared vita and passio, plus the discrete seventy-four-line Translation of Thomas Becket (DIMEV 4768) at Thiemke lines 2455-2528. The two birth openings are alternative transmission states of the Life, not two complete independent lives. Thiemke's German headings, title page, and critical apparatus are modeled as editorial commentary rather than medieval work content. The unit is not the whole South English Legendary, the complete 1919 dissertation, or any one manuscript witness.

catalog_summary.title: Life and Translation of Saint Thomas of Canterbury
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English poet or poets and translator or translators
catalog_summary.date: Life composed approximately 1275; Translation present by about 1300; critical edition published 1919
catalog_summary.region: England, with a qualified South-West Midlands literary and dialectal association

titles.value: Life and Translation of Saint Thomas of Canterbury

assertion.notes: The supplied title makes the composite boundary visible; it is not presented as a medieval rubric.

titles.value: Life of Saint Thomas of Canterbury

assertion.notes: Translation here means the elevation and enshrinement of Becket's relics, not linguistic translation.

titles.value: Die me. Thomas Beket-Legende des Gloucesterlegendars kritisch herausgegeben mit Einleitung

scope.description: Title of the scholarly edition represented by CME00142

titles.value: Wolle ȝe nouþe i-heore þis englische tale, þat is here iwrite
Engelond, glad þu beo, for þu miȝt wel eþe
Seint Thomas þis holi mon under erþe lai

assertion.notes: This exact boundary corrects the initial assumption that CME00142 ended before the discrete Translation.

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English Becket poet or adapter
notes: No colophon or incipit names an author. Individual candidates, including Robert of Gloucester, remain speculative.
The Life freely adapts the Latin Quadrilogus and independently incorporates legal material.

agents.name: Anonymous poet of the Translation of Thomas Becket
notes: The identity of the Translation poet and the relationship to the Life poet remain unresolved.

agents.name: Hermann Thiemke
notes: Editor and collator of the thirteen-witness critical edition published in 1919.

agents.name: Carl Horstmann
notes: Editor of the Laud Misc. 108 text used by Thiemke for siglum H.

agents.name: William Henry Black
notes: Editor of the Harley MS 2277 text used by Thiemke for siglum W; Black's historical Robert of Gloucester attribution is not adopted at work level.

assertion.notes: Robert of Gloucester and other named candidates are not securely identified.

scope.description: Free Middle English verse adaptation of the Latin Quadrilogus with additional legal material

assertion.notes: No physical Quadrilogus exemplar has been identified.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous author or reviser of birth-story version a

assertion.notes: Thiemke argued on metrical grounds for a different author of version a; current authorship remains unresolved.
The poet is anonymous; identity with the Life poet is not established.

scope.description: The 1919 critical representation, including alternate openings, running text, headings, and apparatus
Thirteen-witness critical apparatus, with direct manuscript copies for M through B and printed mediation for H and W

assertion.notes: Taylor gives approximately 1275 for the South English Legendary tradition. Thiemke's historical proposal of 1270 is retained as a related older argument, not false precision.

date_statements.display: extant in early South English Legendary witnesses dated approximately 1280-1305

assertion.notes: Laud Misc. 108 contains both the Life and Translation and is dated around 1280 by Taylor and 1280-1290 by Thiemke, while its current catalog gives a broader codex/core range. Harley MS 2277 is dated 1295-1305 and also contains both works. The normalized window preserves the attributed dating disagreement rather than silently preferring one cataloging tradition.

places.label: South-West Midlands
notes: A qualified literary and linguistic association, not a proven place of composition.
Thiemke argued more narrowly for Gloucester; the manifest preserves this as a historical claim rather than confirmed geography.

scope.description: Reconstructed literary or dialectal association rather than any one witness's scribal dialect

assertion.notes: The witness tradition ranges across later manuscripts and dialects; properties of Vernon or another codex are not transferred to the original work.

form_statements.description: Rhymed long-line couplets, conventionally described as septenary verse

scope.description: The two intrinsic medieval works are verse; prose occurs only in the separately modeled editorial framing and apparatus.

assertion.notes: Taylor argues that the legend was composed as a romance, disseminated as a saint's life, and read as history; the term does not reduce the work to a modern secular-romance category.
The poem adapts a source framed as history and incorporates legal documents into its narrative of church and crown.

summaries.value: A long Middle English verse account of Thomas Becket's legendary parentage, youth, career as royal chancellor and archbishop of Canterbury, conflict with Henry II, exile, return, martyrdom, miracles, and posthumous cult. The accompanying Translation narrates the elevation and enshrinement of Becket's remains. The Life combines hagiography, romance, and historical writing: its foundational source is the Latin Quadrilogus, while its account of the Constitutions of Clarendon also draws on legal materials not present there.

CME00142 serializes Thiemke's 210-line birth-story version a, 156-line version b, a shared Life continuation represented by 2,302 running line elements, and the 74-line Translation at lines 2455-2528. A valid reading path chooses one birth version and yields 2,458 Life line elements, including four alphabetically numbered insertions at 2406a-d, followed by the 74-line Translation: 2,532 verse-line elements in total. The XML physically contains 2,742 running L elements because both alternative openings are serialized; six further L elements occur inside apparatus notes and are excluded from that running-text count.

Thiemke based the b opening and shared continuation primarily on Ashmole 43 (M), but the mutually exclusive a opening follows the Laud-family H text through Horstmann 1887; no witness supplies both openings. He copied and collated ten further manuscripts directly and reached Harley MS 2277 (W) through Black 1845. The lineage manifest keeps these scoped and mediated routes distinct. Modern DIMEV records 6687 and 1507 describe the Life's Laud and Harley redactions, while record 4768 identifies the discrete Translation embedded at the end of the encoded text and supplies a broader collateral witness census than Thiemke's editorial corpus.

Work, redaction, edition, witness, facsimile, encoding, and apparatus layers are separate. The two birth versions are alternative openings of one Life; the Translation is a second medieval work. German is confined to Thiemke's editorial layer. Manuscript dates, dialects, damage, and provider image licenses describe witnesses and surrogates, not the abstract medieval works.

extent.description: Two medieval verse works are represented: the Life and the seventy-four-line Translation. The logical catalog extent follows one coherent Life reading path: 2,458 Life line elements plus 74 Translation lines, or 2,532 in total; four are Thiemke's alphabetic 2406a-d insertions beyond canonical numbering to 2528. The XML physically serializes both alternative birth openings and therefore has 2,742 running L elements plus six variant L elements inside notes. Four LG elements carry running text: two for birth version a, one for birth version b, and one shared container that begins with the common Life and continues across the line-2455 Translation boundary. Two further LG elements with six L elements are nested in apparatus notes. Its full structural count is 2 DIV1, 3 DIV2, 6 HEAD, 6 LG, 2,748 L, 2,699 NOTE, 186 PB, 464 MILESTONE, and 14 P elements, including two direct Anglo-Norman or mixed Latin-Anglo-Norman witness rubrics.

content_parts.label: Life of Saint Thomas of Canterbury
content_parts.summary: The Becket vita and passio, represented by one of two alternative birth-story openings followed by a shared continuation ending immediately before the relic-translation narrative.

locators.value: choose birth version a or b, then continue through line 2454; four additional lines are numbered 2406a-d

notes: The 2,458 count is a logical reading-path count, not the total number of serialized Life L elements.
No single verse-group count applies to both coherent paths: version a uses two opening LG containers before the shared container, while version b uses one.
DIMEV 6687 and 1507 catalog the Laud and Harley redactions of this Life.

content_parts.label: Birth-story version a
content_parts.summary: The 210-line Laud-family opening beginning with an invitation to hear the English tale and narrating Gilbert Becket and Becket's mother.

content_parts.label: Birth-story version b
content_parts.summary: The 156-line Harley-family opening, which passes continuously into the shared Life at line 157.

content_parts.label: Shared Life, Passion, and miracles
content_parts.summary: The common continuation covering Becket's youth and offices, the church-crown conflict, exile, martyrdom, miracles, royal penance, and the deaths of the murderers.
notes: For a version-a reading path, lines 157-210 at the start of this serialized section overlap the conclusion of version a and are skipped; version b uses the section continuously from line 157.

content_parts.label: Translation of Thomas Becket
content_parts.summary: A discrete seventy-four-line relic-translation narrative recounting the long burial, elevation, procession, and enshrinement of Becket's remains.

locators.value: ETS/EEBO/TEXT/BODY/DIV1/DIV2[3]/LG/L from L[@N='2455'] through the final running L

notes: The XML has no new HEAD or independent LG at line 2455: the Translation begins mid-container in the same running-text LG as the end of the Life. The exact DIMEV 4768 incipit and seventy-four-line extent establish the work boundary.

content_parts.label: Laud-family witness rubrics retained by Thiemke
content_parts.summary: Two direct prose rubrics from Laud Misc. 108 frame the version-a opening: one introduces Thomas's parentage and one introduces the Life proper. Their Anglo-Norman and mixed Latin-Anglo-Norman wording belongs to medieval witness paratext, not to Thiemke's German commentary or to the intrinsic language facets of the two verse works.

locators.value: version a: rubric before line 1 and rubric between lines 202 and 203
BODY/DIV1/DIV2[@N='a']/P[1-2], excluding their editorial NOTE descendants

notes: French and Latin are not promoted to work-level language facets because these are witness rubrics represented inside the modern edition layer.

content_parts.label: Thiemke's title-page framing, headings, and critical apparatus
content_parts.summary: German editorial framing and 2,699 notes recording manuscript variants, lineation, sources, and explanatory commentary; the two direct medieval witness rubrics are modeled separately.

locators.value: title page and editorial matter embedded on printed pp. 1-185
TEXT/FRONT, BODY headings and HEADNOTE paragraphs, and all NOTE descendants; excludes the two direct witness-rubric P elements

notes: The six L elements inside NOTE are variant verse and are not part of the running text.
The dissertation introduction is not encoded.

related_works.title: South English Legendary
notes: The Becket Life and Translation circulate within a mobile, multi-witness legendary tradition and may also have had an independent prehistory.

related_works.title: Quadrilogus (Latin composite lives of Thomas Becket)
notes: The Life is a free verse adaptation of the Quadrilogus tradition. The precise redaction and physical exemplar remain unidentified; the relation does not apply wholesale to every added legal passage or necessarily to the Translation.

related_works.title: Constitutions of Clarendon (1164) and supplementary clauses of 1169
notes: The Life incorporates and rearranges clauses from the 1164 constitutions and 1169 material not present in the Quadrilogus; no single surviving exemplar combines the sources in the poem's order.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00142.xml, at CME submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Primary pinned repository evidence for identifiers, Thiemke's title and editorship, Middle English running texts, separate editorial-language layers, alternate opening structure, critical lineation, the line-2455 Translation boundary, apparatus, and printed-page sequence.

evidence.citation: Programmatic structural and line-path audit of CME00142.xml.
evidence.locator: DIV1, DIV2, HEAD, LG, L, NOTE, PB, MILESTONE, and P counts; ancestor::NOTE filtering; line-number discontinuities
evidence.summary: Confirms 2 DIV1, 3 DIV2, 6 HEAD, 6 LG, 2,748 L, 2,699 NOTE, 186 PB, 464 MILESTONE, and 14 P elements. Four LG are direct running-text verse groups: two in version a, one in version b, and one shared group that continues across the editorial Life/Translation boundary through line 2528; two further LG containing six L are nested inside the apparatus note at line 2454. The 2,742 running lines comprise version-a 210, version-b 156, shared Life continuation 2,302, and Translation 74, including four added lines 2406a-d. Two direct medieval rubric P elements frame the a opening; other prose P elements are editorial or nested apparatus.

evidence.citation: Hermann Thiemke, Die me. Thomas Beket-Legende des Gloucesterlegendars kritisch herausgegeben mit Einleitung, Palaestra 131 (Berlin: Mayer & Müller, 1919).
evidence.locator: complete volume; especially introduction pp. I-IX, XLI-LXX and critical text pp. 1-185; PDF pp. 5 and 85-269 for the encoded title page and text
evidence.summary: Primary edition evidence for thirteen witnesses and consultation routes, Ashmole 43 as base, redaction families, editorial criteria and lineation, free adaptation of the Quadrilogus, historical authorship and localization arguments, and the exact text encoded by CME00142.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, records 6687, 1507, and 4768, IMEV/NIMEV 4171, 907, and 3064, version 1.0 data release.
evidence.locator: Records.xml records 6687, 1507, and 4768 and linked manuscript records
evidence.summary: Establishes the Laud and Harley Life redactions, discrete seventy-four-line Translation, incipits, subjects, couplet form, and manuscript loci. Record 1507 lists sixteen Life witnesses; record 4768 has fifteen active Translation occurrences in fourteen physical manuscripts because Rawlinson poet. 225 contains two copies. Bodley 779 appears only as a ghost and Lambeth 223's commented-out unrelated locus is not counted as an active DIMEV occurrence. The duplicated Laud Misc. 463 Life locus conflicts with manuscript-specific evidence and is not repeated as fact.

evidence.citation: Tristan B. Taylor, Thomas Becket in the South English Legendaries: Genre, Materiality, and Why the Reader Matters (PhD dissertation, University of Saskatchewan, 2023).
evidence.summary: Supports approximate 1275 composition, anonymous and speculative authorship, the Laud/Harley/Translation distinction and witness counts, Quadrilogus dependence, romance-hagiography-historiography hybridity, and the independent incorporation of the 1164 and 1169 legal clauses.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for CME00142.
evidence.locator: entities, relations, access, rights, editorial_practices, evidence, and open_questions
evidence.summary: Binds the metadata to the repository XML, U-M encoding, Thiemke edition, two printed intermediaries, Thiemke's thirteen-witness editorial corpus, the broader DIMEV collateral census, complete and partial manuscript surrogates, dated negative access findings, request routes, and scoped text-versus-image rights evidence.

open_questions.question: Can the Life's poet or poets, the author of birth-story version a, the Translation poet, or the original place of composition be identified more securely than anonymous authorship and a qualified South-West Midlands association?
next_steps: Consult post-2023 linguistic and South English Legendary scholarship.
Keep original-language reconstruction separate from the dialects of individual witnesses.
Do not revive Robert of Gloucester or a Gloucester place of composition without new direct evidence.

open_questions.question: Which Quadrilogus redaction or physical exemplar underlies the Life, and through what copies did the poet obtain and combine the Constitutions of Clarendon and 1169 clauses?
next_steps: Collate distinctive readings against Quadrilogus I and II manuscript traditions.
Compare the poem's legal sequence with surviving 1164 and 1169 documentary witnesses.
Retain work-level source relationships without inventing a physical source object.

open_questions.question: Was the seventy-four-line Translation composed by the Life poet, appended by a later poet, or independently attached to different Life redactions?
next_steps: Compare meter, diction, and textual affiliations across all Translation witnesses.
Study manuscript junctions, rubrics, blank space, and item order at the Life-to-Translation boundary.
Preserve the DIMEV work distinction while leaving personal identity open.

open_questions.question: How should Thiemke's 2,532-line-element reading path, canonical numbering to 2528, four 2406a-d insertions, and witness-specific omissions and additions be compared in downstream editions?
next_steps: Record canonical numbers, alphabetic insertions, serialized XML counts, and witness line counts separately.
Do not expose the 2,742 serialized running-line count as if it were one continuous medieval reading path.
Use explicit part and redaction labels in generated publications.

notes: The exact line-2455 match to DIMEV 4768 establishes that CME00142 includes the Translation even though the XML does not introduce a new heading there.
The two birth-story versions are alternate Life openings. They must not be summed into a fictitious single medieval narrative.
Thiemke's direct-versus-mediated witness routes are preserved in the lineage manifest rather than flattened into thirteen direct consultations.
German editorial prose and apparatus do not change the medieval work-level form from verse or add German to the catalog summary's intrinsic language codes.
No manuscript date, dialect, image license, or damage statement has been transferred to the abstract composite work.
